speaker: interviewer
text: All right, Ruth, you were telling us about the DP camps--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and learning how to sew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me. What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when we start to learn how to sew, we got paid cigarettes-- five, six pack a day. And I used to save the money-- sell them. DP camps-- I was pregnant. I have a very bad pregnancy. When my son was born, I still was working.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1946, September 30th.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Saul. And the nicer thing is, I was pregnant seven months till I went to the doctor. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I walk in to the doctor. And I say to him, doctor, everybody gets more stuff-- different food. And I'm pregnant.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he say-- he said, where you carry your baby-- in your head? I says, no-- in my stomach. He say, how more-- or far you are? I say, seven months. He said, I can't believe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, you better touch it. You'll see the baby moves around. He say, maybe five, four, but not seven. I was very small. I no ate nothing. And I think I have now a biggest stomach when I went deliver my son, as he gave me the-- some-- a little note. I can get, in the kitchen, more, like, milk, food. I not drink the milk, but I cook something, you know what I mean-- my mother-in-law, my husband. And camed-- I was there-- learned how to sew. I delivered my-- yeah-- came before Rosh Hashanah-- it was the holiday-- Jewish holiday. I came to the synagogue, and everybody say, oh, she came.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, sure, why not? I say, I'll see you Yom Kippur. I said, I don't think so. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I say, why? I say, I don't think so. And the nicer thing was, five days before Yom Kippur, my son was born. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I-- I came-- I was playing cards with the guys Sunday night. I washed the clothes. I felt I need to do things. I need-- I was ready to go-- of mine count, not of the doctor's count-- of nobody's. I counted myself. And comes to the-- at about 3 o'clock, I was good-- run and then with the water. And I said to my husband, we need to go in hospital, here, in the camp. I think I go deliver the baby. And he say, fine. I went. And I came there. And the doctor was there. And I say, I need to go in hospital, for I think I am in pain to deliver the baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: He say, OK. You want me to send you to the main hospital? I send you. Well, I no think so.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, fine. I went. He went to put a note to give the driver, to give the doctor-- I'm not ready to deliver a baby. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went in the hospital there, and the doctor was not smart to keep me. He just sent me back. And I went in a room. It was like 14 patients there with babies. That-- that the month was-- everybody having babies. And I went-- seen a friend. I know the friend delivered a baby. And I went there. And she-- and I talked to her. And I say to the nurse, nurse, I think I need to go back. But I can't go myself. I'm very sharp pained. She says, OK. I take you. And I went to the delivery room. And she say, you go take a shower. And I stepped-- high step-- the bathtub-- and the water break-- broke. And she got scared. And she hollered for the doctor. And an hour later, I delivered my son. Coming out the-- as the doctor said to my husband, well, I screamed not for my mother. I just screamed, dad, help me. And I delivered the baby. The doctor walked out to my husband. He say, you have a son! And there was another guy. He brought his wife a day before, and she not doing. He said, that's my son. He said, oh, no, that's his son. He say, another thing I tell you, Dave. You need to name the baby after her father. For she no hollered for her mother. She hollered for her father. He say, fine. And that's what I-- give the name-- mine older son-- after my father. I came home. I no have a no diapers-- no nothing! I brought home the baby. The DP camp handled a few diapers, a few things.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I have, again, no money. I spent the money. On what? I bought other stuff for-- that I needed. [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know what. I bought a couple things, and I went in hospital-- no money.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came back. Two weeks I was home with the baby. I said to my mother-in-law-- I say, you watch the baby. I go work back.

speaker: interviewee
text: She say, she's not watching the baby. She's not a babysitter. She said, watch?

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, fine. I went to a next room, to the neighbor. And her daughter have a baby-- the same thing. And she watched her daughter's baby-- an older lady.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, you want get paid to watch my baby too? She say, no problem. And she watched the baby-- mine.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went back to work. And after, when I have the baby, we was better workers already. We knowed more sewing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the guy say, well, you would you like to work piecework? I say, fine with me. And I start to work piecework-- started working from 8:00 till 5:00. I'd get home 2 o'clock, 1 o'clock. I was fast with a machine.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that-- till-- happened that, like, to 1949-- '48. End of '48, we got-- Truman took over. Roosevelt not let us in the United States. Truman-- when Roosevelt died, Truman took over, and he opened the doors. And I got a letter from mine uncle when my baby was a year old, I think. I put it in the papers, in the forwards-- 'looking for family.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And mine uncle find me. And he sent a letter.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: William-- William Weitz-- Dave Weitz, William Weitz-- they find me here-- there. And they send me papers to come. But Roosevelt-- that was '46-- '47. He no let nobody in. When Truman took over, I went to Munich, to the consul, and I say, I have the papers. I want to go to United States to mine uncle. Mine uncle pays for everything, I say. You don't need to worry. He say, we'll see what we can do. That was already '48. '49, March the 9th-- was '49-- in the beginning, you need to go to American doctors-- consuls-- American consuls. I went in a consul. It's an American. And it was a Polish guy. He talked to me Polish. I answered him Polish. He asked me all kind questions. And I need to know, from 1936, what happened to me, to 1949. And one-- one thing he-- he say to the-- to that guy were-- was-- telling him-- him-- he say, ask her how she survived in hiding woods. I said, tell him I went stealing. Well, it's true! And I don't know, at that time. He say, she was a crook. And the guy say-- the judge said, you was a crook. I say, tell the judge, when he would be hungry, he would be a crook, too! And he said, she have a big mouth! Nah, she can't go to United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: He's laughing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's true--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --swear to God.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you arrive in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1949, March the 9th.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you come straight to Pittsburgh?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to New York. Mine uncle came to New York and brought me to Pittsburgh. And I stayed six months with mine uncle. We cannot '49, get an apartment or stuff.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I got an apartment in Dawson Street, and when I saw the apartment, again I start. I cried a whole night. I get up in the morning. Mine uncle says to me, what happened to you? Your eyes are red. I say, I have a headache. And I won't tell him. And when I saw the apartment, how bad it was looking-- paper was hanging, the woodwork was black. It was on Dawson Street off-- it was corner of Dawson and Edward. And-- and I say to myself, how people can live like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my husband came. He got a job by suitcases-- Weinstein's Suitcases-- work-- for $25 a week-- $0.75 an hour-- in '49. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I saved up a few dollars. And I start to working by my aunt on a brand new sewing machine. I came to my aunt of the third floor, and I saw the sewing machine. The sewing machine was rusted-- cannot move. I took apart, and I asked mine uncle, do you have a gasoline to put of the sewing machine-- the rust shouldn't eat up. When he saw I took apart the sewing machine, he say, oh my God. I call a guy what knows how to fix it. I said, don't worry. I put the sewing machine back together. I know how. Well, there, the guy what taught us to sew, he taught us to fix a machine ourself. It was a pedal. It was not a electric. And I fixed the machine, and my aunts, mine cousin, brought clothes to fix it. There was nobody ask me to-- you need to pay. I was working for six months for nothing. When I moved in, August the 15, in Dawson Street, I make myself a couple dresses. I went in [NON-ENGLISH], bought material-- $0.39 a yard-- and make sundresses. It was now-- it was summertime. And I was watching mine son. He was 2 and 1/2 years old. September, he's supposed to be 3.

speaker: interviewee
text: Came up a lady to me, and she say to me, I love your dress. Can-- where you bought it? I say, I made it. She say, can you shorten me a couple dresses? I say, why not? But you need to pay. She says, sure. I'll pay. I say, $1.50 per hem. And she make me, of Dawson Street, a dressmaker-- the lady. Her daughter is still coming to me for 49 years. OK? And that's what happened here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after 1949-- '54, I got to be a citizen. Lawrence forked us in 500 people in a soldier memorial. And I bought the house in '54-- moved in here. And I got stuck with $3-- left with $3-- three-- two kids-- a boy, 8, a boy 4-- no-- a boy 18 months and 8.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was your 18-month-old? What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- the middle boy was Bobby.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born April '53-- 19-- April 19, '53.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was he named after?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was named after my father-in-law and after my mother-- he was named.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have another son, in-- in '57-- Harold.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the date of his birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was June 15, 1957.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was he named after?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's named after my brother and after my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what was your husband's occupation, after--

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he came here. He used to work by the suitcases, by Weinstein. After that, I moved to Newcastle. Mine uncle thought I go move with them. And I say, nothing doing. He worked in Southern Avenue, in a bar-- in an restaurant. He used to call [NON-ENGLISH]. He used to make $50 a week. But he worked seven days-- six days, seven days a week-- night. He used to get up at nighttime, till my son, my youngest son was born. Five weeks later, the [NON-ENGLISH] burned down-- got no job. I have three kids. I bought a new stove I need, payments, a new carpet. There was a guy. His name was Mr. Siegel. He used to have a-- a store in Edwards Street-- furniture store. And I need to make payments to him-- not to the bank. He never send me to the bank. He was very nice with me. And no job-- I was working with three kids, mortgage payment, furniture payment, and no money to come in. He used to get from this-- from the employment, $35 a week, my husband. I can't go far with $35 a week. But I survived. I was working. But I have very lovely customers, here. That-- see, that time used to come doctors' wives. And they never left. And I start fixing clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? The doctors used to charge you $3 for a visit, not $130. See, now I can see them. They leave everything in the stores. They have lots of money to pay.

speaker: interviewee
text: That time, a doctor's wife-- she no go spend for a hem $10 when she can get it for $3. And her husband needs to see 10 patients to make $30. It was a different life.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So you were sewing. You were still living in this house?

speaker: interviewee
text: I live in this house for 42 years now--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --never moved no place.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And your husband-- did he get another job after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: He got a job working in a cleaning store in Hampstead. He used to make $30 a week. He not work 40 hours. I mean, he worked 40 hours, he made $50. Then it paid, like, $1.10 an hour.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me about your religious beliefs after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: I believed-- well, I was not so religious before the war. I used to ride Saturday. We never used to ride before the war. I-- I never cooked Saturday-- never cooked. I never-- I mean, shop too much. I used to go-- going downtown, shopping. I buy like, clothes, but not for food the whole week. And I believe I go to the holidays, synagogue. I believed in the memorial service, I go. And-- and I believe that you need to keep something.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Are you active in any Jewish organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I'm too active-- too much organizations. You want me to figure out every one?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell. Well, just tell us a little bit about what you do.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hadassah, B'nai B'rith, Na'amat, Ladies Hospital Aid, [NON-ENGLISH] Sisterhood, [NON-ENGLISH] Sisterhood-- that's a lot of members everyplace; Old Age Home. Holocaust in Pittsburgh, Holocaust in California, and Holocaust in Washington Museum.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's OK? Now--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever make it to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was, once, 18 years ago. I went to Israel-- four weeks there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you-- did you ever regret that you didn't go to Israel instead of the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- never regret. While I was in Israel, and we're not happy with Israel-- was that time. I don't know why. I-- I was in the-- in the Holocaust there. And when I saw the camps, and I saw the city where they put it, when a lady don't catch me, I was for sure was-- thrown back.

speaker: interviewee
text: A lady stayed with me. I say, oh my God. Where they-- how'd they figure this out? How they figured?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went through. I donated there money to put it in the book-- my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: At Yad Vashem.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was there. And that-- so I came home. I was four weeks in Israel. I traveled. I was in Solomon's dome. I was in Eilat. I was-- I mean, my-- I went with a group. I was by Dave's tombstone, Sarah's tombstone.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went in the Bethlehem. We went-- I have a nice trip. It cost too much money--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK

speaker: interviewee
text: --that trip.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your sons receive a Jewish education?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They went in Hebrew school, and they was bar mitzvah-- every one. And it was very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And can you tell me a little bit about the later events in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I'm working. I'm going out. And I have a good time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband died, 1940-- '61.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And how old were your sons when your husband died?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son was-- the older one-- 14, my younger was 8, and my younger-- 8, the middle one-- and the younger one was 4.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And how did you support yourselves after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I support where I worked. We working. And I got a little bit money from Germany, from the-- well, I were-- you know, gutmachung.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wiedergutmachung-- mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I got from my-- I fight very hard, and I got for my husband back pay, and I got back paid. 19-- 1965 my son signed up-- he was 19-- and went to the navy, and went to Vietnam War. That was a disaster-- for two years. After, when he came back, he went in college. And I fight for me to get money from the gutmachung. '67, November, I got a check for $36 a month. When I saw that check-- $36 a month-- I was so excited. I get more money-- clear money-- $36 a month! That was a lot! That paid my light bill! I was still $36 a month.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I got paid back. And that helped me-- Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to pull through my son. I helped a little bit-- not too much-- my son in college. The only thing what was good-- he was home. And he-- and not-- give me nothing, no money, for support.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: This way, he went in college-- Point Park-- four years college and two years in Duquesne for his master's. 1970, he got a job-- Pittsburgh Plate Glass.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are your sons married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two married. Mine older one have two children. And one is David. They're named after my husband and after my daughter-in-law's father. And one is named after her grandpa. And just I picked a name-- Andy-- Andy Samuel Weitz-- that's the youngest one, and the older one, David Herbie Weitz. And mine youngest son have a little girl named Tyrany Sara, after my mother-- the second name. They're very gorgeous three grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. All right. Is there anything that we have left out of your story that you would still like to tell us about?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that's plenty.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And can you tell me, is there any lesson to be learned from your experience? Is there anything that you would like to-- a message? A--

speaker: interviewee
text: A message? I would tell the people-- you cannot give up. You need to fight for your life. And you never stop. Anything what happen to you, you cannot give up. God, I didn't know what happened, last year, to me. January the 15-- the 15, I walked-- took a walk, where I walked every day from my house to Giant Eagle. It was two blocks. And I fell and broke the hip. I'm still not giving up. I still going places. I do places. I travel. And my kids remember me well. They don't let me alone. They take care of me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. That's it. Good! All the pictures I got back, I get-- when I was in concentration camp, I get my father's friend to keep it for me. And when I was liberated, he brought to me the pictures back. One load-- one load-- a lady burnt all the pictures-- a neighbor-- the best neighbor, my-- us. We lived together. We was playing girls together. And she burned all have pictures. It's just a few-- the pictures what I have in here is the neighbor-- was a-- a friend of my father's. He kept it for me. That's what happened. That was my father-- 1936-- took the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my mother and me. I was four years old-- and when we took pictures home. That was 1926. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrific. I can't believe. That's my sister here when we went in a group-- belonged to Haganah. And we took pictures. But I'm not in that picture. I missed that. My sister, my boyfriend, here, my cousins-- 2, 3, 4 people-- 5-- about 5 people still living. And the rest-- German Hitler took them. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was-- that's mine picture, Ruth Weitz. I was about 20 years old, in concentration camp-- 1942. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That's my brother. He was killed, 1943, end of July. He was 13-- never reached his 14th birthday. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That picture was taken 1938, before the war. He was in the Polish army. He was in the concentration camp. He wasn't hiding woods. He saved my life. He took care of me. He was killed, 1945, February the 14. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that's me-- that was me, 1947, in DP camp-- Leipheim-- taken, the picture. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That is-- was me and my husband, 1946, in the DP camp-- it was taken. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was me, my husband, and my son when we came to United States, 1949. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That was my husband-- camed-- come DP camps, in 1946, after the war-- '46. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now I have them-- all my family; mine older son, Saul; my younger son, Harold; mine middle son cannot be here; mine-- my daughter-in-law, Cassie; my daughter-in-law Nancy; mine grandson, the older grandson, David; my younger-- middle son-- grandson, Andy; and my youngest granddaughter, Tyrany Sara Weitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, I'd just like to take this opportunity to thank my mother for the wonderful job that she's done. As a survivor, OK, she came to this country with really nobody or nothing. And she raised us. She did it by herself, with very, very little help from anybody. And I think she's done a great job. Unfortunately, my father died very, very young. He was a survivor, as well. And we all miss him. We all love him. But we love our mom. She's done a great job. Thanks. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you want me to say something, Cathy? What? Oh, we love our mother. She did do a great job. She gave us everything we needed. She did a great job. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] Good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thanks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
